digest.intro: The ceasefire is dead, the ocean is cooking, and Adobe finally lost one.

items.title: Hormuz at 28% — The MOU That Became a Tripwire
items.blurb: The April ceasefire is functionally over: Hormuz tanker traffic has collapsed to 28% of pre-war baseline (15.6 ships/day), the US and Iran are both publicly claiming control of the Strait while conducting fresh strikes, and the interim MOU's Paragraph 5 — meant to guarantee safe passage — has instead become the legal pretext each side uses to justify the next escalation. Iran detained Ahmadinejad on Mossad-recruitment charges, which signals serious internal fracture; Israel's cyber ops are still running despite the ceasefire. Neither side has an exit that doesn't cost them politically, so they're both performing resolve while the shipping lanes empty out.

items.title: Graham Gone, War Powers Filed — The Senate Math Just Shifted
items.blurb: Lindsey Graham, who built the Senate coalition supporting the Iran strikes, died suddenly of an aortic tear at 71 — and Schiff immediately filed a War Powers Resolution, sensing the arithmetic changed. Trump told Fox News 'we're taking over the Strait of Hormuz' the same morning Iran's supreme leader adviser said retreat 'has no place in the mind of any friend of Iran,' which tells you how far apart the two sides actually are despite Trump's claim that 'everything was agreed to' in an 11-hour Sunday meeting.

items.title: The Graph That Should Be Front-Page News
items.blurb: The Niño 3.4 sea-surface temperature record isn't just setting a new high — it has departed entirely from the observed range since 1982, meaning this year's line sits outside every single prior year's envelope on the chart. This is direct satellite and buoy measurement, not a model or forecast. Goldman is pricing a 15.8% food commodity surge on top of the energy shock already running through Hormuz, and 10,650 Europeans have already died in the June heatwave — the two crises are now compounding each other in real time.

sections.heading: Tools, trust, and the AI credibility war

items.title: Zig Creator Calls Spade a Spade, Anthropic Blows Smoke
items.blurb: Andrew Kelley publicly named Anthropic as an unreliable narrator whose '$1 trillion IPO valuation depends on selling hypothetical future impact' — the essay frames Anthropic's 'coding is going away' narrative not as prediction but as investor storytelling with real downstream damage: architecture and staffing decisions made from fear rather than evidence. 720 points on HN means this landed; the piece is really about how credibility moves in technical communities when a major lab needs a story more than it needs to be right.

items.title: Robin Sloan at the March to Stop the AI Race
items.blurb: Sloan walked from OpenAI's office to Anthropic's HQ in San Francisco and explains exactly why this particular protest earned his presence: the demand is unusually concrete — every major AI lab CEO must publicly commit to pausing frontier development if every other major lab credibly does the same. What makes it worth reading isn't the politics but Sloan's observation that the weird thing about the AI debate is that no one, not even accelerationists, actually disputes the basic fact pattern — powerful technology, unknown mechanisms, profound economic effects, no planning horizon — they just draw opposite conclusions from it.

items.title: I.J. Good's 1964 Paper on the First Ultraintelligent Machine
items.blurb: Good's thesis — 'the survival of man depends on the early construction of an ultraintelligent machine' — was written at Oxford before integrated circuits were common, and his core chain (a smart machine designs a smarter machine, recursively) is exactly the argument being re-litigated right now. He also noted in 1964 that we'd need to understand the human brain to make a real breakthrough, and we still don't — which either means LLMs are something else entirely or we're pattern-matching our way toward something we can't fully see. Reading present-day fears through 60-year-old speculation has a clarifying effect.

sections.heading: Letters, marks, and the physical act of making

items.title: From Bull's Head to 'A' — How an Ox Became the Alphabet
items.blurb: The Latin 'A' is a stylized ox head rotated 90 degrees: aleph, drawn by Bronze Age traders in the Sinai, had two horns and a triangular face — tip it over, add a crossbar to hold the diagonals apart, and you have the skeleton of every 'A' you've ever typed. The rotation wasn't aesthetic; it followed the shift from carving into stone (where vertical strokes dominate) to writing on papyrus (where horizontal motion is faster). What's quietly remarkable is the chain: Phoenician abjad → Greek vowel reassignment (a cognitive leap the essay calls 'ergonomic') → Etruscan → Roman stonecutters rationalizing the form for chisels.

items.title: Backtrack-Free Cursive — Engineering the One-Stroke Word
items.blurb: The author noticed that English cursive requires backtracking — lifting or reversing to dot i's and cross t's — in 51% of words, averaging 0.68 backtracks per word, while Russian needs it in only 6.4% of words (0.066 per word), a difference he quantified by running Crime and Punishment through both languages. The essay is ostensibly about designing a pen-path algorithm for a backtrack-free cursive system, but it's really forcing you to think about how letterforms encode sequence and connection — the hidden temporal logic inside a static visual form.

items.title: BumpMesh Is Way Better — Physical Texture on 3D Prints
items.blurb: CNC Kitchen doesn't hype tools without reason, and half a million views on a single feature video is a genuine signal. BumpMesh applies physical surface textures to 3D prints — generative surface aesthetics that live at the physical/digital boundary — and it's free, which means the barrier between seeing it and using it on your next print is basically zero.

items.title: SFPD Drone Footage Leak — What a City Looks Like When the Watchers Aren't Watching Themselves
items.blurb: Leaked footage from SFPD's Skydio X10 program shows four separate drones coordinating to follow a single suspect across San Francisco over the course of one hour — tracking his license plate, following passengers who got out of his car, and filming his takedown from two simultaneous angles — all triggered by an alleged theft of car parts. The perceptual shift here is the key thing: at 200 feet with a locked thermal-optical zoom, the city becomes a continuous graph of movement with no ambient cover, and the surveillance was only revealed because someone leaked the video rather than through any disclosure process.

facts.fact: Hormuz tanker traffic has fallen to 15.6 ships per day — 28% of its pre-war baseline — approaching what analysts describe as 'closure-level' traffic.
The Latin letter 'A' is a Bronze Age ox head (aleph) rotated 90 degrees — the rotation followed a shift in writing tools, not aesthetic preference.
Writing Crime and Punishment in English cursive would require backtracking in 51% of words; in Russian, only 6.4% — because Russian has almost no letters requiring secondary strokes.
I.J. Good published his 'intelligence explosion' thesis — that a sufficiently smart machine would design a smarter machine, recursively — in 1964, before integrated circuits were commercially common.
Four separate SFPD drones coordinated to follow a single suspect across San Francisco in under an hour — the operation was triggered by a suspected car-parts theft.

art_picks.title: Sans Soleil
art_picks.creator: Chris Marker
art_picks.why: A film that operates as a feedback loop between memory, image, and meaning — Marker wanders Tokyo and Guinea-Bissau asking how cultures construct time and ritual from the same raw material of living. The essay-film form is itself a generative system; watching it makes you want to make something immediately.

art_picks.title: Mutant / Arca
art_picks.creator: Arca
art_picks.why: Arca's music sits exactly at the edge where the body and machine become indistinguishable — sculpted, broken, tactile sound that reads like generative 3D rendered as grief and desire. The physical-digital boundary doesn't just blur here; it becomes the subject.

art_picks.title: Today series (1966–2013)
art_picks.creator: On Kawara
art_picks.why: Every day for nearly five decades, Kawara painted the date he was alive in the local language — nothing else. It's unreasonably deep craft in service of a brutal feedback loop between existence, ritual, and systems of measurement, the kind of obsessive constraint that should make any builder stop and reconsider what they're actually making.

events.name: Nicola Vassell Gallery: Follow Through (Group Show)
events.venue: Nicola Vassell Gallery, 138 Tenth Ave, Chelsea
events.why: A tight group show including Alteronce Gumby, Ming Smith, Alberta Whittle, and Uman — artists working across painting, photography, and installation whose practices center identity as system and material simultaneously.

events.name: Gabriel Orozco: Public Nature
events.why: Orozco's first large-scale US public art commission — 12 trompe l'oeil photographs on 300 bus shelters exploring how nature and infrastructure quietly reshape each other; free, ambient, you walk into it.

events.name: SummerStage: Mavis Staples / Brother Wallace
events.why: Free outdoor show with Mavis Staples — one of the last living links to both the civil rights movement and the deep architecture of American gospel-soul; the kind of performance that makes genre feel like a weak word.

events.name: SummerStage: Anjunadeep Open Air NYC
events.why: A benefit concert bringing Anjunadeep's deep/melodic electronic programming outdoors into Central Park — James Grant & Jody Wisternoff headline, with a full slate including Marsh and Durante; atmospheric music in an improbable green context.

events.name: MoMA PS1 Warm Up 2026 — Opening Night (Carlos Souffront, Wackies Showcase with Lloyd Barnes & Mark Ernestus, TELESONIQ)
events.venue: MoMA PS1, 22-25 Jackson Avenue, Long Island City, Queens
events.why: The opening night pairs The Bunker NY's Carlos Souffront with a rare Wackies reggae showcase and Basic Channel's Mark Ernestus — a lineage-tracing set that maps dub, Detroit, and underground NYC into a single courtyard.
